metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.run_git` can serve as the reconciler's git adapter (it already uses `-C <cwd>` + timeout + no-shell); if yes, import it rather than creating a second `git_adapter` module — if the reconciler's isolation boundary prevents importing from `rebar._store`, document that constraint explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a brand-new `git_adapter` module without acknowledging that `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py` (`run_git`) is an already-shipped, production-grade git-subprocess adapter with 10 import-sites. Introducing a second adapter for the reconciler risks NIH duplication and splits the codebase's git-invocation policy across two owners.
findings.location: What section: 'Introduce one `git_adapter` module (mirroring `acli`)'; also SC6 call-sites list
findings.suggested_fix: Check whether `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` is importable from the reconciler's runtime (it's stdlib-only, no circular deps). If yes, replace the 'new module' AC with 'reconciler git calls route through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git`'. If not (e.g., engine isolation hard rule), add a single sentence to the plan explaining why a new module is required despite the existing one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the relationship between the new `git_adapter` and the existing `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py`: either extend/reuse `gitutil.py` for the reconciler (noting the package-boundary constraint), or explain in the plan why a separate adapter is warranted and document the intended long-term ownership boundary.
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge the existing `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py`, which is already a centralized git subprocess wrapper (`run_git(cwd, *args, ...)`) with identical motivation and shape. Creating a new `git_adapter` without explaining how it relates to `_store/gitutil.py` leaves two parallel central git seams in the codebase.
findings.location: What section: 'Introduce one git_adapter module (mirroring acli)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and evaluate `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.run_git` (and `_ref_lock._git`) as prior art before committing to a new `git_adapter` module — either reuse/extend `gitutil` (if the stdlib-only engine constraint permits it), or explicitly document why a new module is required (e.g. the reconciler engine cannot import from `rebar._store`) and how it relates to the existing wrappers.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py` already provides `run_git(cwd, *args, ...)` — a thin, uniform `-C <cwd>` git subprocess wrapper with the same design goals as the proposed `git_adapter`. The plan does not mention this prior art or explain why it cannot be reused, extended, or shared with the reconciler engine instead of introducing a new parallel module.
findings.location: ## What / ## Why — proposed `git_adapter` module; prior art: `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or characterization test requirement) that executes a git operation through the new adapter with `repo_root` set to a path that differs from the process CWD and asserts the correct result — proving the -C fix end-to-end, not just structurally.
findings.finding: The plan claims to 'fix F14 at the seam' via the git_adapter cutover, but no acceptance criterion exercises the corrected -C/repo_root code path end-to-end against a real git repository. All stated verification is structural (code ownership, no-direct-subprocess grep). A structural check cannot prove the CWD bug is actually resolved — the adapter could pass -C incorrectly and all grep-style ACs would still pass green.
findings.location: AC4: '_attestation.py no longer owns raw git (SC7); git verify uses the adapter (fixes F14 at the seam)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'An integration/characterization test invokes at least one git_adapter op (e.g. verify_commit or log_format) with repo_root != process CWD and asserts success, demonstrating the -C flag resolves F14.' This can be a pytest fixture that creates a temp git repo, changes CWD to a sibling directory, and calls the adapter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the git plumbing ops used in `__main__.py`'s legacy-lock-file purge (`cat-file`, `read-tree`, `rm --cached`, `write-tree`, `commit-tree`, `update-ref` with CAS) to the adapter's op enumeration, or explicitly declare that migration in scope with a note on how the adapter handles the GIT_INDEX_FILE env override.
findings.finding: The adapter's enumerated op set (verify_commit, log_format, rev-parse, add/commit/push, ref-lock ops) omits the advanced git plumbing ops used in __main__.py: cat-file, read-tree, rm --cached, write-tree, commit-tree, and update-ref with CAS — a 7-call sequence for the legacy lock-file purge migration (lines 185–244). These calls must either be wrapped by the adapter or explicitly excluded.
findings.location: What section: 'exposing the git ops actually used (verify_commit, log_format, rev-parse, add/commit/push, ref-lock ops)'; Citations: '__main__.py'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or adapter design note) specifying that the git_adapter is a thin pass-through with no retry/buffering for lock-protocol calls (`_advisory_lock.py`, `_ref_lock.py`), and that error propagation is unchanged.
findings.finding: The plan migrates locking-protocol git calls in `_advisory_lock.py` and `_ref_lock.py` through the new adapter, but does not specify that the adapter preserves the atomicity of lock-then-act sequences (e.g., no retry, buffering, or swallowed errors that could silently break lock invariants). If the adapter adds uniform error-handling or retry around git ops, it could change the failure mode of a lock acquisition, creating a TOCTOU or silent-unlock-on-error scenario.
findings.location: Scope / cascade_flag; AC line 2 ('no direct git subprocess remains'); SC6 sites `_advisory_lock.py`, `_ref_lock.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'For lock-protocol operations (advisory_lock, ref_lock), the adapter MUST propagate non-zero exit codes unchanged and MUST NOT retry; a distinct `git_lock_op()` entry point (or a `raise_on_error=True` default) enforces this.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `_ref_lock._git()` is in scope for migration; if so, specify how its domain-specific RefLockTimeoutError translation and binary-stdin blob-write semantics are preserved through the new adapter (or explicitly exclude it from the 'no direct git subprocess remains' AC).
findings.finding: `_ref_lock.py` already contains its own `_git()` seam with domain-specific behavior (RefLockTimeoutError translation, binary stdin for `hash-object`, `text=False` for blob content) that cannot be mechanically replaced by a generic adapter without losing those semantics. The plan lists `_ref_lock.py` as one of the `~8 modules` to migrate but does not address this specialization.
findings.location: SC6 citation: `_ref_lock.py`; cascade_flag; AC line 'The ~8 modules call the adapter; no direct git subprocess remains in them'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `git_adapter` accommodates `_ref_lock.py`'s domain-specific contract: the adapter must support binary-mode (`text=False`) and raw `stdin` for blob ops, and must let `CalledProcessError`/`TimeoutExpired` propagate unmodified so `_ref_lock` can continue translating them into `RefLockTimeoutError`/CAS discriminator outcomes — OR document that `_ref_lock._git()` is intentionally kept internal and not migrated to the shared adapter.
findings.finding: `_ref_lock.py` already owns a domain-specialized `_git()` internal function that translates `TimeoutExpired` → `RefLockTimeoutError` and relies on `CalledProcessError` from `check=True` for CAS discriminator logic, and accepts binary `stdin`/`text=False` for blob operations. The plan lists `_ref_lock.py` as a migration target without specifying how these domain-specific error-translation contracts are preserved when delegating to a generic `git_adapter`. A naive adapter migration (wrapping `subprocess.run` uniformly) will break the CAS error classification and blob-ingestion path unless the adapter's interface is designed to support binary mode and let `CalledProcessError` propagate unmodified.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 2 ('The ~8 modules call the adapter; no direct git subprocess remains in them'); `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py:240-266`
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Scope or What section, add: '`_ref_lock._git()` is a domain wrapper that translates `TimeoutExpired` → `RefLockTimeoutError` and relies on raw `CalledProcessError` for CAS discrimination; the `git_adapter` must expose a pass-through mode (no error translation, binary stdin support) so `_ref_lock` can route through it without losing its error contracts — OR explicitly exclude `_ref_lock._git()` from the migration while still eliminating the direct `subprocess.run` at the other call sites.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or test-design note specifying that at least one integration test exercises the `git_adapter` against a real (temp) git repository to verify that `-C <repo_root>` routing works end-to-end, not solely through mocked subprocess assertions.
findings.finding: The plan relies on 'characterization tests before/after' without specifying whether those tests will exercise real git subprocess calls or mock subprocess.run. If the tests mock subprocess (the likely path for unit tests), the core change — routing all callers through `-C <repo_root>` in the adapter — is never executed in the test suite. This is the proxy-validation anti-pattern for the bug-fix path being defaulted to.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 and cascade_flag section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'at least one integration test uses a real temp git repo fixture (e.g., via pytest tmp_path + git init) to confirm adapter ops succeed with an explicit repo_root, covering the F14 scenario end-to-end.' This is sufficient to guard against proxy-validation without requiring full live Jira/GHA integration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe AC5 as an observable outcome: specify at minimum which behaviors must be characterized (e.g. verify_commit pass/fail, commit-back happy path, ref-lock acquisition) and that the test suite passes identically before and after the migration (`pytest tests/reconciler/characterization/ -v` green on both the pre- and post-migration commits).
findings.finding: AC5 ('Reconciler behavior unchanged (characterization tests before/after)') is effort-framed rather than outcome-framed: it describes an activity ('write characterization tests, run before/after') without stating what observable outcome constitutes success — no framework, no coverage scope, no specific behaviors to characterize, no pass/fail criterion.
findings.location: AC5: 'Reconciler behavior unchanged (characterization tests before/after)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5 with: 'Characterization tests covering [verify_commit, log_format, ref-lock acquire/release, binding-store commit-back] pass on the pre-migration codebase and pass unchanged on the post-migration codebase (`pytest tests/reconciler/characterization/ -v` green in both states); no behavioral diff is observed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the characterization-test AC to enumerate the minimum required scenarios (at minimum: git op failure/non-zero exit, missing/wrong repo_root, and each migrated module's primary call path).
findings.finding: The AC for behavioral equivalence ('characterization tests before/after') does not specify what scenarios or call paths the tests must cover, leaving the proof of behavioral equivalence unverifiable. With ~8 modules migrated, a partial characterization could miss edge cases (e.g., non-zero exit from git verify, missing repo_root, advisory-lock contention paths).
findings.location: AC line 5: 'Reconciler behavior unchanged (characterization tests before/after)'; Scope section

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `git_adapter` functions should make `repo_root` a required (non-optional) parameter to fully close F14, or whether the `repo_root=None` CWD-fallback is intentionally preserved for specific callers — state this explicitly in the AC or What section so the implementer doesn't inadvertently carry the bug forward.
findings.finding: The plan states `_attestation.py`'s `verify_attested_commit` 'fixes F14 at the seam' via the adapter, but the existing code already accepts `repo_root: Path | None = None` and already applies `-C` when provided — it only fails when callers pass `repo_root=None` (the default, which preserves CWD-relative behavior). The plan does not specify that the adapter migration should make `repo_root` non-optional (i.e., remove the `None` escape hatch), which is the actual F14 closure. Without this, an implementer preserving the optional signature in the adapter leaves F14 open for any caller that still passes `None`.
findings.location: AC line 4 ('`_attestation.py` no longer owns raw git (SC7); git verify uses the adapter (fixes F14 at the seam)'); `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_attestation.py:91-119`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 4: 'The adapter's `repo_root` parameter is required (not optional), ensuring every call site is forced to supply an explicit path — callers that previously relied on CWD are updated to pass the resolved `repo_root` value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the git_adapter API contract in the plan: at minimum, whether ops raise on non-zero exit or return a result object, and what the timeout policy is — so that 'behavior unchanged' is verifiable against a known spec.
findings.finding: The git_adapter's API contract is not specified — return types, error-handling policy (exceptions vs return codes), and timeout behavior are left for the executor to choose. Since all ~8 modules must migrate to this adapter, an undecided API contract is a non-trivial design decision that could require rework if different callers need different error semantics.
findings.location: What section: 'Introduce one git_adapter module... exposing the git ops actually used'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'API contract' note, e.g.: 'Each adapter function raises subprocess.CalledProcessError on non-zero exit (matching current call-site behavior); returns stdout as str; accepts an optional timeout_s defaulting to 30s. Callers that currently check return codes will be wrapped to preserve that behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned update to CLAUDE.md or an ADR capturing the `git_adapter` convention (all reconciler git I/O must go through it, explicit repo_root required) so the pattern is discoverable and enforceable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (a central `git_adapter` seam mirroring `acli`) but includes no planned update to CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or a design doc — leaving future contributors without guidance on the intended pattern or when to add new git ops to the adapter vs. inline.
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item: 'CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) updated with the git_adapter convention: all reconciler git subprocess calls must route through git_adapter with explicit repo_root; direct git subprocess calls in reconciler modules are disallowed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git_adapter` error-handling contract in the plan: define timeout defaults, how non-zero exit codes are surfaced (exception type, logged stderr), and whether any ops retry — and add an AC item verifying this policy is applied consistently.
findings.finding: The plan centralizes git error-handling and timeout policy as a motivation but specifies no concrete policy for the adapter — no timeout value, no error-surfacing contract (exception type vs. return code vs. stderr capture), and no retry/backoff decision — leaving the AC silent on what 'correct' error handling looks like.
findings.location: ## Why / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'git_adapter raises GitAdapterError (wrapping CalledProcessError + stderr) on non-zero exit; all calls pass a configurable timeout (default: 30 s); no automatic retry (callers decide). Adapter logs stderr at WARNING on failure.' Then add a corresponding AC item.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that 'behavior unchanged' means functional reconciler outcomes are preserved, NOT that the F14 CWD bug is preserved — and add an explicit AC or test that verifies correct CWD behavior (i.e., the right repo_root is passed and used) as a distinct, positive criterion separate from the characterization suite.
findings.finding: The Scope section and AC5 state 'behavior must stay identical' / 'Reconciler behavior unchanged (characterization tests before/after)', but the Why section and AC4 assert the plan 'fixes F14' — a CWD bug that produces incorrect behavior. Characterization tests that lock in current behavior would canonicalize the buggy CWD behavior, preventing the F14 fix from being validated or even allowing it to pass.
findings.location: Scope ('Behavior must stay identical') vs Why ('root of the F14 CWD bug') vs AC line 4 ('fixes F14 at the seam') vs AC line 5 ('Reconciler behavior unchanged')
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, change 'Behavior must stay identical' to 'Reconciler functional outcomes must stay identical; the F14 CWD fix is the sole intended behavioral correction.' Add an AC: 'A test confirms git commands receive the correct repo_root via -C (F14 regression test).' Update AC5 to note that characterization tests cover everything except F14-affected paths, which are covered by the new regression test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between AC4 ('fixes F14') and AC5 ('behavior unchanged / characterize before/after'): add an explicit behavioral assertion that the adapter passes `-C <repo_root>` at the subprocess boundary for the F14 scenario (not a snapshot), separate from the structural characterization that non-F14 output is preserved.
findings.finding: The AC simultaneously states 'Reconciler behavior unchanged (characterization tests before/after)' AND 'fixes F14 at the seam' — these are contradictory. Characterization tests that snapshot current (buggy) behavior before the fix, then verify the same output after, will tautologically 'pass' by locking in the F14 bug. This is the self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: the before-snapshot captures wrong behavior, and the after-check validates it is still wrong.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('fixes F14 at the seam') vs AC5 ('Reconciler behavior unchanged (characterization tests before/after)')
findings.suggested_fix: Split the testing approach into two distinct concerns: (1) a targeted behavioral test asserting that every adapter call site invokes git with `-C <repo_root>` — this is the F14 correctness check and must NOT be a snapshot of previous behavior; (2) characterization/integration tests (ideally using a real temp git repo, not mocked subprocess) verifying that high-level reconciler outputs are preserved for cases unaffected by F14. Explicitly note in AC5 that 'behavior unchanged' excludes the F14 bug fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the SC5 citation from `reconcile.py:259-299` to the correct location of `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` (currently starting at line 131) so implementers find the actual binding-store git code.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'SC5 inline commit-back — `reconcile.py:259-299`' but the actual `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` function starts at line 131 in the current file, not line 259. The stated line range does not contain the binding-store git code; a reviewer or implementer going to lines 259-299 will not find the cited code.
findings.location: Citations section: 'SC5 inline commit-back — reconcile.py:259-299'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the citation to: 'SC5 inline commit-back — `reconcile.py:131-215` (`_commit_binding_store_snapshot`; hardcoded `.bridge_state/bindings.json`, `.bridge_state/bindings-retired.json`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the SC5 line citation from `reconcile.py:259-299` to `reconcile.py:131-214` (the `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` function containing the actual git subprocess calls).
findings.finding: reconcile.py:259-299 is cited for the binding-store commit-back, but that range contains loader bootstrapping code (lazy_load import), not the commit-back. The actual _commit_binding_store_snapshot function is at approximately lines 131–220.
findings.location: Citations section: 'SC5 inline commit-back — reconcile.py:259-299'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Citations section: 'SC5 inline commit-back — reconcile.py:131-214 (_commit_binding_store_snapshot; hardcoded .tickets-tracker and .bridge_state paths at lines 164,169)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or note confirming that `_attestation.py`'s public interface (function signatures, exception types) is preserved unchanged after the git ops are delegated to the adapter, or explicitly list callers that must be updated.
findings.finding: The plan restructures `_attestation.py` by removing its raw-git responsibility, which may change the module's public interface (e.g., `verify_attested_commit` signature or exceptions raised). No AC or body section acknowledges existing callers of `_attestation.py` functions and whether they need updating beyond the adapter migration.
findings.location: What section ('Pulling git out of `_attestation.py` also resolves SC7'); AC line 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC — e.g. for AC2: `grep -rn 'subprocess.run.*["git"' reconciler/ | grep -v git_adapter` should return empty; for AC3: `grep -rn '\.tickets-tracker\|bindings\*\.json' reconciler/` outside the constants file should return empty.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion has a concrete proving command. The ACs state structural outcomes ('no direct git subprocess remains', 'hardcoded paths are named constants') but give no grep pattern, test invocation, or CI check that an executor or reviewer can run to produce evidence of completion. An agent finishing this work has no deterministic signal for 'done'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all five ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append a 'Verification' subsection with: (AC2) `grep -rn 'subprocess.run' reconciler/ | grep -v git_adapter.py` exits 1 (no matches); (AC3) `grep -rn '\.tickets-tracker' reconciler/ | grep -v constants.py` exits 1; (AC5) `pytest tests/reconciler/characterization/ -v` passes before and after migration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out strategy to the plan (e.g., 'the adapter is additive-first: introduce git_adapter alongside existing calls, migrate module-by-module, remove direct calls only after each module's characterization tests pass, with a feature-flag or revert-commit boundary') so the migration is reversible at each step.
findings.finding: The plan migrates internal interfaces across ~8 modules (removing direct subprocess calls, introducing a new adapter boundary) but specifies no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing. Characterization tests detect regressions but do not constitute a reversal mechanism.
findings.location: Scope, AC lines 1–5 (no rollback section present)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Migration sequence' section: (1) introduce git_adapter as a new module with no callers (purely additive, safe to revert); (2) migrate one module at a time, running characterization tests after each; (3) remove direct subprocess calls from each module only after its tests pass; (4) document the single-commit revert boundary per module. This satisfies expand-contract without needing a runtime flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item requiring that `git_adapter` logs each git invocation's failure (stderr, exit code, repo_root, op name) at WARNING or above, so new failure points are observable.
findings.finding: The plan adds a centralized git_adapter wrapping all reconciler git I/O but mentions no instrumentation (logging, metrics, or tracing) for failures at the new adapter boundary, leaving operators blind to git subprocess failures in production.
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'git_adapter logs all non-zero subprocess exits at WARNING level, including: operation name, repo_root, exit code, and captured stderr (truncated to 500 chars).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
